text.content:
Read aloud as the party comes down to the Refectory at the second bell after dawn, the morning after the crane-and-coins envelope arrived at breakfast. Overnight, Caelith brought the evidence up out of the cellar Strong Room and laid it out by candlelight for daylight work.

Morning comes gray over Loftwick.

The Little Palace smells of wet stone, hearth smoke, boiled coffee, and sealed wax. Outside, the city has already begun celebrating a victory it does not understand. Bells ring from somewhere beyond the garden wall. Hooves clatter over street stones. A clerk in the reception hall argues softly with a militia sergeant over who is allowed to enter.

In the hearth room, Caelith Dunivar has laid Haskur Vandrell's possessions across the long oak table.

Not as treasure. As evidence.

Each object rests on a square of linen. Each square is numbered in Caelith's precise hand. The room feels less like a sitting chamber now and more like a battlefield after all the blood has learned to write.

A woman is already at the table. She sits with a small cup of pale tea balanced on one knee. Her dress is the color of a forest at dusk; she wears a small silver leaf at the collar. When you enter she does not stand. She inclines her head a careful inch and sets her cup down.

Caelith looks up.

"I have good news," he says. "The man you fought was almost certainly Haskur Vandrell."

He taps one gloved finger beside the ruined spell focus.

"The bad news is that Haskur Vandrell has been dead before."

The woman beside him speaks before he does, in a voice you have to lean toward.

"Good morning," she says. "I am Trina. I advise Caelith on people. I am sorry about the envelope at breakfast. I expect we will get to it last."

Transitions to: Caelith's expanded explanation of Haskur Vandrell (next page) and the morning evidence list.

GM note. Trina's introduction here is light. She will not steer the morning unless asked. She watches Alicia in particular and offers Alicia a cup of tea without commentary; if Alicia accepts, Trina's quiet mentor relationship begins (see Trina Alvere → Mentor Notes — Alicia).

GM note — location: the morning review happens in the Refectory (M4) — long oak table, hearth-warm room, slate-light through high windows. The evidence stays here for the rest of the morning. The cellar Strong Room (C5) returns to being storage; Caelith carries items back down at the end of each working session.

pages.name: Caelith's Expanded Explanation — Hand of the Duke

text.content:
GM Brief — Phase 2 Purpose

Read this before running the phase. Do not read aloud.

Phase 2 — The Dead Man's Receipts — should accomplish five things, in roughly this order:

  • Confirm that Haskur Vandrell survived by design, not luck.
  • Show that he was part of a larger logistical network, not a lone agent.
  • Introduce Rel Astra as the strongest eastern lead — without yet proving Haskur is there.
  • Create political pressure inside Loftwick through Arthen Moll and the Council's reconciliation faction.
  • Give the party a reason to leave the Yeomanry with urgency, not just curiosity.

The phase ends when the party knows, in their bones, this:

Haskur Vandrell did not flee to safety. He fled to an account someone had already opened for him.

The Speech

Caelith speaks once the party has settled. Read aloud in his voice; cut where the table prefers, but the four bracketed beats below are essential.

"Last night I told you what was true and not for public ears. This morning I will tell you what is true and not yet, by formal record, anyone's. I prefer that we agree on a standard of trust before we keep working."

He places a flat brass token on the workbench between you. Etched on it: a quill above a closed gate.

"I am, by quiet appointment of the Duke, the Hand. The Yeomanry does not maintain that title in any document our Council reads. My office at the Audit Hall is the body of the work; the Hand is the part that goes outside our borders. You have been corresponding with that part since you walked through the Lesser Gate."

[Beat 1 — the title. Caelith gives his real role.]

"Haskur Vandrell is not a man I learned about last week. He has been on the back of my desk for nine years. In that time he has died twice — pulled back, like he was last night, by a contingency someone else laid down ahead of him. The first was at sea, off the coast of Onnwal, in the year 575. He was on a Rel Astran-flagged hull that took on water and he disappeared between deck and boat. We assumed drowned. The second was at a knife-fight in Hardby in 580. He was bleeding out on a tavern floor and a barmaid I had not yet identified put a hand on his chest, and he was not there. When I went to find the barmaid she was, also, not there."

[Beat 2 — the pattern. Haskur is a recurring node, pulled back twice before. The conspiracy is at least nine years old.]

"Each time, we tried to follow the pull. Each time, the line went dead before it reached anyone whose name I could write down. Whoever is at the other end of the contingency does not stop the contingency to give us a name. They run, and they re-cast."

"That is why your fight matters. He is not the man who made him. He is the man someone made, the man someone keeps making. To do that — to underwrite a man twice in nine years and keep him moving between cities and pretexts — costs more than a guild has, and it costs more than a noble cousin has. It costs the kind of money you only see when an institution is being used like a wallet."

[Beat 3 — the scale. The conspiracy is institution-scale, not personal.]

"Last night you saw a letter of credit drawn six weeks before your fight, in Rel Astra, with a marginal endorsement that named him or his agent. This morning, in better light, we have seen a great deal more. I would like to walk you through it. I would like you to tell me what you see that I have not."

"If, after that, you still wish to take the eastern commission, I will tell you the three ways you can travel and the cost of each. If you wish to leave us to our work, I will pay you in full and you will hear nothing from me again. I have said this before; I am saying it again so that, on this morning, in front of Trina, no one mishears."

[Beat 4 — the choice, restated, with a witness.]

GM notes — what to draw out from the speech

  • The brass token. Caelith leaves the Hand-token on the workbench during the rest of the morning. He picks it back up only when the party leaves the room. Players who notice it (passive Perception 14) understand he is showing it to them deliberately, not flashing it.
  • Two prior "deaths." Onnwal coast (575 CY) and Hardby tavern (580 CY) are real prior incidents the GM can spin into Phase 2.5 hooks if desired. Phase 2 itself does not pursue these.
  • The barmaid in Hardby. A loose thread; not resolved. The party may file her name (which Caelith does not have) for later.
  • Caelith does not name the Duke. The Duke's name is for the GM to decide and drop into the campaign. Caelith refers only to "the Duke" or "the Hand," which is sufficient for the moment.
  • Trina says nothing during this speech. She is the witness. If Caelith looks at her at the end, she nods once.

If the party asks the obvious follow-ups

"Why are you telling us this?" — "Because I would like the next person who tries to pull Haskur back to be visible to someone whose name is not in our register, and who therefore cannot be reached by what is in our register."

"Why us specifically?" — "Because you have already done the part of the work that requires being a stranger to the Yeomanry, and because the part of the work I cannot trust to my own clerks is the part where the strangers do the writing."

"Who do you suspect inside your office?" — "I will not name a clerk to you whom I cannot prove. If I named one and you treated them as proven, I would have made you the wrong kind of Hand."

"What is Trina to this?" — "Trina is a private citizen who has spent twenty years funding poets and watching how people enter rooms. She is here because I asked her to come. She will say what she sees when she sees it. She will not say more than that."

What Caelith says plainly during the morning

As the party works through the items, Caelith will say each of these things, unprompted, when the topic comes near. They are the framing he wants the table to land on:

  • "Haskur Vandrell is not a noble, not a priest, not a general, and not a court agent in any ordinary sense."
  • "He is what powerful people use when they need something done, but not known."
  • "A courier. A fixer. A negotiator of debts no honest court would recognize."
  • "He appears in records near failed prosecutions, missing witnesses, spoiled harvest reports, and mercantile disputes that resolved too cleanly."

What Caelith does not yet say

He suspects compromise inside Loftwick. He has no proof. He will not name a clerk to the party until he can. The party will read this hesitation if they look for it (Insight DC 14); they may volunteer to help him find the name. He will accept their help. He will not lead them toward a particular suspect.

Seven items, laid out in order on the workbench. Each builds on what the party already saw last night (see Phase 1 → Caelith Dunivar → Evidence — Haskur's Abandoned Gear). Caelith and Trina both stay in the room while the party works. Trina watches; Caelith answers factual questions and no others.

1. The Burned Spell Focus

The dark-wood ritual focus from last night, but in daylight one face is unmistakably burned — a long scorch mark down the inside of one groove, with a small bead of melted beeswax fused into the char. The focus is intact, but it has been used hard, recently.

  • Arcana DC 13: A focus shows scorch when its caster has channeled a contingency-class spell through it — that is, used it as the conduit of a pre-laid escape rather than as a casting tool. The focus does not normally take heat damage from ritual work.
  • Arcana DC 16: The contingency was not Haskur's own. The melted wax-bead has a sigil pressed into it that does not match the spellbook annotations: a stylized three-rayed sun closing into a fist. Whoever laid the contingency was not Haskur.
  • GM: The three-rayed sun-into-fist is the sigil of the conspirators' patron school of magic. Do not name it in Phase 2; it is a Phase 3+ thread. Players who push for an identification get only: "a school I have seen on two unrelated documents in nine years, and never with a name attached."

Caelith, looking at the focus: "This was not instinct. This was a contract being honored."

2. The False Letter of Passage

From the sealed wallet of correspondence, the second sealed letter (broken open this morning by Caelith). It is a Yeomanry letter of safe passage naming the bearer as Haskur Voren, Agricultural Assessor, in service to the Loftwick Bureau of Crop Estimation. Stamped with a Bureau seal. Signed in a hand that is, on first reading, perfectly correct.

  • Investigation DC 12: The letter is on Yeomanry vellum. The seal is real wax. The chop matches.
  • Investigation DC 15: The signature does not match the current Bureau Chief's hand on file in the Audit Hall. It matches the previous Chief's — who retired in 581 CY. The letter is dated last summer.
  • History DC 14: The Bureau of Crop Estimation does not assign assessors to itinerant routes; their assessors are seated and audit by mailed return. An "assessor" carrying this letter through inns and toll-gates would not be questioned by a Yeomanry watch — but they would be questioned by the Bureau itself if they ever showed up to a real audit. Whoever forged this knew the gap.
  • GM: The forger had real Yeomanry vellum, real wax, the retired Chief's signature on file (or someone able to match it), and the procedural knowledge that this specific cover identity would not bring scrutiny. That is an inside job.

Caelith, dry as winter: "Agricultural assessor… very funny or very grave. We will know which when we know who pays for the harvest he was claiming to count."

3. The Clipped Coin (Eastern Countermark)

The unusual silver coin from last night, viewed by daylight. Its edges have been clipped — shaved with a small blade — to reduce its weight, and on the clipped edge there is a tiny countermark punched into the silver: a tower above three waves.

  • Investigation DC 12: The countermark is a private exchange mark — not state mintage. Someone has "weighed in" this coin against an internal account.
  • History DC 15: The tower-above-waves is an eastern port mark, used by certain Aerdi counting houses on the Rel Astran coast.
  • History DC 17: Specifically the clearing-mark of Cindren & Vhal, Bonded Factors — the same firm whose crane-and-coins seal countersigned the letter of credit last night. The clipped coin is a Cindren & Vhal internal token. Haskur was carrying their accounting, not their bank notes.

Caelith, holding it to the light: "This is not a coin. It is a receipt pretending to be money."

4. The Black-Lacquered Scroll Tube

Inside the brass tube of charts (Phase 1 evidence #6) was a second tube — a smaller black-lacquered cylinder, no longer than a forefinger, sealed at one end with red wax. The wax is unbroken. Caelith has not opened it.

  • Investigation DC 10: The lacquered tube is a courier-grade vessel: shockproof, watertight, and designed to be carried inside another tube as plausible deniability.
  • Arcana DC 13: The wax seal is enchanted with a minor abjuration — anyone who breaks the seal without speaking the correct passphrase triggers a single discharge of command (suggestion: "Forget." Wisdom save DC 13). The discharge is single-use; once a PC fails or succeeds, it is spent.
  • Arcana or History DC 17: The abjuration glyph itself, and the lacquer-and-inlay work on the tube, both read as elven craftsmanship — but in a style no Yeomanry sage can place. The proportions of the glyph nodes, the way the lacquer is laid down in seven thin coats rather than the conventional four, the way the inlay traces unfamiliar hands rather than leaves: all of it is elven, and none of it is from any cantonal school anyone present recognizes. The most accurate sentence anyone can offer is, "Elven. But peculiar."
  • Inside, after the seal is broken: a single strip of vellum bearing four words (see #5), and a fragment of ledger paper (see #6).
  • GM: Whichever PC opens the tube triggers the command. If they fail the save, they spend their next turn following the suggestion (likely losing a round of evidence handling, possibly setting down what they were holding). It is not lethal; it is administrative. This is the mood of the conspiracy.
  • GM (campaign-level): The tube and its seal are not elven. They are Drow work. In this world Drow are an ancient legend no one alive remembers, so the best identification anyone can make is "elven, but peculiar." Do not name "Drow" in Phase 2 under any roll. If a PC casts legend lore or similar, the response is a long fragmentary verse about a people who walked downward and were forgotten, which the caster cannot place. The peculiarity is the entire reveal in this phase.
  • Investigation DC 14: The interior of the tube has been deliberately wiped clean. There is no residue of any third item beyond the vellum strip and the ledger fragment — but there was something in there once, and it left a faint heat-darkened smudge on the inner lacquer.
  • Arcana DC 15: The smudge is consistent with heat-reactive ink — a message that was meant to be read once, by someone who knew to warm the surface, and then to disappear. Whatever was in there has been read, removed, and burned. The tube was not just a container; it was a relay.

Caelith, snapping the tube shut between two fingers: "The container was bait. Whatever was in there before the seal was last set, we will not know without the man who set it."

5. The Vellum Strip — "HARBOR BEFORE THRONE"

Inside the lacquered tube, a strip of fine vellum the width of a finger. Four words in clear, careful capitals: HARBOR BEFORE THRONE. No date, no signature, no other ornament.

  • History DC 13: The phrasing is older than current Yeomanry idiom. It reads as a clause from a charter or a pre-Aerdi succession formula.
  • Religion DC 15: The phrase appears in two surviving fragments of the Aerdi Concordat of the Bay (4ᵗʰ-century Aerdi imperial settlement law) — a constitutional principle stating that a port-city's harbor council was to be seated before any newly-named throne could collect its revenues. In short: the harbor outranks the crown until the crown formally acknowledges the harbor.
  • Investigation DC 14: Read as code rather than law: an instruction to seat (or seize) the harbor before any new throne can claim its dues. A timing instruction, not a slogan.
  • GM: The line is a signal of intent. The conspirators are timing something against an unnamed succession. Do not resolve in Phase 2 what throne or what harbor. The party should leave the room with the phrase memorized and uncertain.

Caelith, half-smiling without amusement: "Power flows through ports before crowns notice. That is older than the Yeomanry, older than Rel Astra, possibly older than thrones. It is also a slogan, which is what people write down when they intend to do something the law will not yet name."

6. The Ledger Fragment

Also inside the lacquered tube. A torn corner of double-entry paper, no header, no date. Three lines, in three different hands:

VANDRELL settled
MORAVEN delayed
GILST transferred

  • Investigation DC 13: The three lines were written by three different clerks. This is not a single person's notes; it is a status board, kept somewhere a small group of people pass through.
  • Investigation DC 14: "Settled," "delayed," and "transferred" are accounting verbs, not personnel verbs. Each refers to the disposition of an account or instrument. Cross-referenced with the letter of credit (Phase 1 evidence #4): "VANDRELL settled" matches the date Haskur's escrow was drawn down. The clerk who wrote that line had real-time access to Cindren & Vhal's settlement records.
  • Investigation DC 16: "MORAVEN delayed" refers to a held instrument awaiting countersignature. "GILST transferred" refers to an account that has been moved off one ledger and onto another — and, in clerks' shorthand, sometimes refers to a person whose payroll has been quietly migrated to a different paymaster.
  • Investigation DC 14, on the paper itself: there are no sums anywhere on the fragment. No amounts, no dates, no totals. A real settlement ledger would carry figures on every line.
  • GM: These three names — Vandrell, Moraven, Gilst — drive the Phase 2 investigation. See Phase 2 Investigation for the records-alcove research that identifies Cindren & Vhal, Tamsin Moraven, and Merro Gilst.

Caelith, after a long silence: "There are no sums. That means the money was not the point. People were the point. They were tracking who was where, and who was no longer."

7. The Hidden Seed Pouch

This morning, Caelith found a small cloth pouch sewn into the lining of the spellbook's back cover. He cut the lining open in front of the party. Inside: a small handful of grain seeds — wheat by appearance — and a folded scrap of paper bearing one line in tiny script: "Seed for the dry barony."

  • Nature DC 13: The seeds are durum wheat, but they are blighted at the husk — sterile. They will not germinate. They are display seeds, not viable stock.
  • Nature DC 16: Sterile durum wheat is sometimes used in false crop claims — a pouch shown to an inspector to evidence a harvest that does not exist. The seeds prove origin (durum, not winter wheat) but cannot be planted to verify the claim.
  • Investigation DC 14: "The dry barony" is not a Yeomanry phrase. It does not match any registered Yeomanry estate. It does match an old colloquial name for a region of the South Province of the Great Kingdom known for poor rainfall and grain-import dependency. A "dry barony" would be a customer for forged crop receipts on grain that does not exist.
  • Nature DC 17 or Investigation DC 16 with herbalist's tools: The seeds have been chemically treated — a thin alchemical wash across the husk that would, on cursory inspection by a low-grade assessor, make a sterile grain look freshly viable. Whoever made this pouch wanted it to pass a quick eye, not a serious one. The treatment is itself a forgery.
  • GM: The seed pouch ties Haskur to a false crop scheme — selling grain that does not exist, on paper, to buyers in a region that cannot verify. The verb "delayed" on Tamsin Moraven's line is now ominous: a grain factor who could expose a false crop scheme has been "delayed" in the firm's accounting.

Caelith, fingering the treated husks: "Either disguise… or truth wearing a mask. I cannot decide which is more frightening."
